rewrites.newText: She was making a lighthearted joke about her hardships and did not truly mean it
rewrites.targetsMisreading: tone misread — reading a sad, modest remark as humour
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage frames the quote as spoken 'with characteristic modesty' and expressing genuine hurt ('used me so unkindly'), not as a joke

rewrites.newText: She believed her own lack of education was the reason she never received proper recognition
rewrites.targetsMisreading: self-blame misattribution using a detail from the wrong paragraph
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 4 explains her lack of education and lack of recognition, but the final quote blames the world for treating her unkindly, not herself

rewrites.newText: She was warning other women that a career in science would only bring them disappointment and mistrust
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right theme (women in science, paragraph 4) but wrong point — treating a personal reflection as advice to others
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the quote is Mary describing the effect on herself, not a warning addressed to other women

rewrites.newText: She had become so bitter that she had completely given up on other people and abandoned her work
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-reading — inflating 'suspicious of everyone' into total bitterness and surrender
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'suspicious' is milder than 'given up entirely', and the passage stresses she kept working with fierce determination, so 'abandoned her work' contradicts the text

rewrites.newText: The complete skeleton of an Ichthyosaurus
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusing a genuine discovery for the exception in a NOT question
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraphs 1 and 3 describe Mary uncovering the Ichthyosaurus skeleton, so it IS one of her discoveries and cannot be the correct 'NOT' answer

rewrites.newText: The first complete Plesiosaurus, a long-necked reptile
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusing a genuine discovery for the exception in a NOT question
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 6 states she made the first complete Plesiosaurus discovery, so it IS one of her discoveries

rewrites.newText: The first British example of a flying Pterodactyl
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusing a genuine discovery for the exception in a NOT question
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 7 states she discovered the first British Pterodactyl, so it IS one of her discoveries

rewrites.newText: To persuade readers to visit the Jurassic Coast where her statue stands
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from paragraph 11 (Jurassic Coast, statue) mistaken for the whole purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage is a biography celebrating Mary and the unfairness she faced, not a tourism advert; the coast and statue are one closing detail

rewrites.newText: To explain how fossils form in the Blue Lias limestone of the Dorset cliffs
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from paragraph 2 (Blue Lias limestone) mistaken for an explanatory purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage never explains the process of fossil formation; the limestone is mentioned only as where Mary hunted

rewrites.newText: To describe the different prehistoric creatures whose fossils were found along the Dorset coast
rewrites.targetsMisreading: content-focus confusion — naming creatures is not the author's purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the creatures illustrate Mary's achievements; the passage's aim is to celebrate her and expose her unfair treatment, not to catalogue prehistoric animals

rewrites.newText: To argue that women make better scientists than men and should replace them in the field
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-reading the unfairness theme into a claim the text never makes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage argues Mary was unfairly overlooked, never that women are superior scientists or should replace men

rewrites.newText: The two teams finished the breakthrough far ahead of the planned schedule despite all the earlier delays
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported detail — assuming the moment was remarkable because it beat the timetable
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage stresses the project ran late and over budget (paragraph 9), so 'ahead of schedule' is unsupported and contradicts the text

rewrites.newText: The final section of rock was broken through at exactly midnight while millions of people watched on television
rewrites.targetsMisreading: fabricated precise time detail (over-literal on 'nerve-wracking moment')
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage gives the date (1st December 1990) and says it was broadcast live, but never states the breakthrough happened at midnight

rewrites.newText: The workers had to bore their way through unexpectedly hard rock that the boring machines struggled to cut through
rewrites.targetsMisreading: contradiction — imagining hard rock when the rock was chosen for being soft
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 1 describes the chalk marl as a soft rock the machines cut through easily, so 'unexpectedly hard rock' contradicts the passage

rewrites.newText: A rare and precious treasure that everyone wants to own and admire
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal/opposite reading — 'white elephant' sounds valuable and desirable
rewrites.wrongnessReason: in context (paragraph 9) the critics used the term to dismiss the project as a wasteful failure, the opposite of a prized treasure

rewrites.newText: A powerful symbol of the lasting friendship that formed between Britain and France
rewrites.targetsMisreading: importing the paragraph 12 friendship theme into the idiom's meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the phrase is used by critics who dismissed the project, so it carries a negative meaning, not a celebration of friendship

rewrites.newText: An enormous and impressive structure so large that it dominates the whole landscape around it
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading — 'elephant' equals something huge
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'white elephant' refers to a costly useless project, not to physical size; the critics meant it as an insult about waste

rewrites.newText: A construction project that takes far longer to finish than anyone had originally planned or expected
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right domain (delays were mentioned) but wrong meaning of the idiom
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a white elephant is an expensive, useless outcome; slowness is not the idiom's meaning, so this is the right topic but the wrong point

rewrites.newText: Because they were small and furry like the animals they were named after
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal transfer of a real mole's traits onto the machines
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 5 describes each TBM as weighing over a thousand tonnes and as long as two football pitches, so 'small and furry' is the opposite of the truth

rewrites.newText: Because moles are blind and the machines could operate in complete underground darkness
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible-sounding inference the text never licenses
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the metaphor is drawn from a mole's burrowing action through soil, not from blindness or a preference for darkness

rewrites.newText: Because they were designed by a famous engineer whose surname happened to be Mole
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal name-origin fabrication
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage gives no such inventor; 'mechanical moles' is a comparison to the burrowing animal, not a person's name

rewrites.newText: To explain why the Channel has always been such a dangerous stretch of water for boats
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal reading of 'wrecked ships' as the point of the opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the wrecks are evidence of how formidable the barrier was, used to make the tunnel more impressive, not a standalone warning about boating danger

rewrites.newText: To provide a detailed history of the many naval battles and wars fought across the Channel
rewrites.targetsMisreading: 'defeated armies' expanded into a military-history purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage is about building the tunnel; the armies and ships only establish the barrier's difficulty, not a history of warfare

rewrites.newText: To argue that a bridge across the Channel would have been a far better solution than the tunnel
rewrites.targetsMisreading: seizing on 'bridging the gap' to invent an argument the text never makes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage never argues a bridge would have been better; the opening establishes the barrier, not a case against the tunnel

rewrites.newText: To show that the tunnel was completely unnecessary because ships had already been crossing the Channel for centuries
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported inference that the tunnel was pointless
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage celebrates the tunnel as a triumph; the opening shows the barrier's difficulty, not that the tunnel was needless

rewrites.newText: The Channel Tunnel will soon be replaced by a bridge linking the two countries
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported prediction seeded by earlier mentions of bridging
rewrites.wrongnessReason: nothing in the passage suggests demolition or a bridge replacement; the tunnel is presented as a lasting success

rewrites.newText: Eurotunnel will build a fourth tunnel beneath the Channel to cope with increasing passenger demand
rewrites.targetsMisreading: extrapolating from the three tunnels to an invented fourth
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage describes exactly three tunnels and never hints at a fourth being planned or built

rewrites.newText: Passenger numbers will steadily decline in the coming years as more travellers choose to fly instead
rewrites.targetsMisreading: prediction that contradicts the growth trend in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 10 reports over twenty million Eurostar passengers a year and faster journeys, pointing to continued importance, not decline

rewrites.newText: To suggest that we should stop exploring space until we understand our oceans
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported opinion read into a factual comparison
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the Mars comparison only highlights how little we know about our oceans; it never argues against space exploration

rewrites.newText: To explain why so many deep-sea creatures look strange and alien to human eyes
rewrites.targetsMisreading: content from later paragraphs (odd creatures) grafted onto the Mars comparison
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the Mars line is about the gap in our knowledge, not about the appearance of the creatures

rewrites.newText: To prove that exploring distant planets like Mars matters more than exploring our own deep oceans
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reversing the author's point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the comparison actually implies our own oceans deserve more attention, the opposite of prioritising Mars

rewrites.newText: To argue that Mars might once have had deep oceans similar to the ones on Earth
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading of the Mars link as a claim about Martian water
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Mars is used only as a yardstick for how little we know about our seas; the passage makes no claim about oceans on Mars

rewrites.newText: The freezing temperatures near the ocean floor stop most animals from being able to eat properly
rewrites.targetsMisreading: cold is mentioned but wrongly blamed for the food shortage
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage attributes scarcity to the absence of sunlight and therefore plants (paragraph 2), not to temperature stopping animals eating

rewrites.newText: Human pollution reaching the deep ocean has killed off most of the organisms that once lived there
rewrites.targetsMisreading: pollution (the plastic bag, paragraph 5) misused to explain scarcity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: pollution is noted as a sad sign of human reach, not as the cause of food scarcity, which is the lack of sunlight and plants

rewrites.newText: There are far too many hungry predators all competing with one another for the same limited supply of food
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible ecology guess the passage never supports
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the text explains scarcity through the lack of a plant food base, not through overcrowding of predators

rewrites.newText: Both have already been thoroughly explored and mapped by scientists
rewrites.targetsMisreading: false claim of shared exploration
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage says Europa's ocean is only believed to exist and most of Earth's ocean floor is unexplored, so neither is thoroughly mapped

rewrites.newText: Both are warmed by heat coming directly from the sun above
rewrites.targetsMisreading: contradiction — assuming the sun heats both
rewrites.wrongnessReason: vents are heated by the Earth's crust and Europa by gravitational forces; paragraphs 6 and 8 make clear neither relies on the sun

rewrites.newText: Both are home to exactly the same species of heat-loving bacteria
rewrites.targetsMisreading: overspecific claim about shared species
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage describes vent bacteria on Earth but only speculates that Europa might harbour life; it never says they share species

rewrites.newText: Both were first discovered by scientists during the very same year of exploration
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misapplying the 1977 vent date to Europa
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 1977 refers to the Earth vents' discovery; Europa is discussed as a possible ocean world, with no shared discovery date given

rewrites.newText: To frighten the reader by making deep-sea creatures seem like terrifying monsters of the deep
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misreading the author's purpose as scaremongering
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the anglerfish is used to illustrate bioluminescence as a survival tool, not to frighten the reader

rewrites.newText: To compare the anglerfish and its glowing lure with similar hunting creatures found on land
rewrites.targetsMisreading: inventing a land comparison the passage never draws
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage discusses the anglerfish only as a deep-sea example of bioluminescence and makes no comparison with land animals

rewrites.newText: To explain why the anglerfish is the most dangerous and deadly predator in the deep sea
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported superlative about danger
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage never ranks the anglerfish as the most dangerous predator; the detail serves to show how bioluminescence aids survival

rewrites.newText: That marine biologists like Sylvia Earle are the most important and valuable scientists working anywhere in the world today
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-reading the Sylvia Earle quote into a claim about scientists' status
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the quotation supports protecting the ocean; the final paragraph's message is about the resilience of life and conservation, not the standing of marine biologists

rewrites.newText: That the creatures living in the deep ocean are far more dangerous and threatening to humans than scientists had ever thought
rewrites.targetsMisreading: carrying an earlier 'danger' impression into the closing message
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the final paragraph highlights how resilient and inventive life is and why it should be protected, not that the creatures threaten humans

rewrites.newText: It creates a calm and peaceful atmosphere that makes the deep ocean feel gentle and welcoming
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite tone from the harsh, hostile opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the opening describes crushing pressure, darkness and near-freezing cold, creating an alien, forbidding mood, not a calm one

rewrites.newText: It makes the reader feel guilty about all the pollution that humans have dumped into the deep ocean
rewrites.targetsMisreading: importing the pollution theme from paragraphs 5 and 10 into the opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the opening paragraph mentions no pollution; it evokes curiosity about an extreme, alien world

rewrites.newText: It bores the reader by listing far too many dry statistics and numbers about the depth of the ocean
rewrites.targetsMisreading: negative reaction unsupported by the vivid opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the opening uses vivid imagery ('crush a submarine like a tin can') to intrigue, not a dull list of statistics

rewrites.newText: It confuses the reader by using far too many difficult scientific terms before the topic has even been introduced
rewrites.targetsMisreading: assuming jargon-heavy confusion in an accessible opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the opening uses plain, dramatic description with no technical terms, so it does not confuse the reader

rewrites.newText: A scientific explanation of how volcanoes form and why they erupt
rewrites.targetsMisreading: volcano content mistaken for the text type
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage narrates a historical event and its rediscovery; it never explains the scientific process of how volcanoes form

rewrites.newText: A personal diary entry written by a resident who lived through the eruption
rewrites.targetsMisreading: the Pliny eyewitness detail mistaken for a first-person diary
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage is a third-person factual account, not a personal diary; it merely quotes an eyewitness within that account

rewrites.newText: A persuasive text urging readers to help fund the preservation of the ruins of Pompeii
rewrites.targetsMisreading: the conservation content of paragraph 11 mistaken for a persuasive purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage reports conservation challenges factually; it does not urge or persuade the reader to take action

rewrites.newText: That Roman children were badly behaved and often scribbled rude words all over the walls of the city
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail about children from paragraph 1 misattributed to the graffiti
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the graffiti reveals ordinary Romans' everyday thoughts and feelings; it is not evidence about the behaviour of children

rewrites.newText: That the Romans had no proper system of writing and could only scratch simple pictures onto their walls
rewrites.targetsMisreading: contradiction — treating graffiti as proof of no writing
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the graffiti is itself writing (jokes, complaints, declarations), so it disproves rather than shows an absence of a writing system

rewrites.newText: They have completely run out of the funding needed to continue digging
rewrites.targetsMisreading: funding mentioned for restoration misused as the reason for leaving sites buried
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage says sections are left deliberately so future technology can recover more; it does not blame a lack of funding

rewrites.newText: The sections that remain buried are not thought to contain anything interesting or valuable
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported assumption that the buried areas are dull
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage gives no such reason; the areas are preserved precisely because they are valuable to future excavators

rewrites.newText: The Italian government has passed a law that forbids any further digging at the site
rewrites.targetsMisreading: the government's restoration funding twisted into a legal ban
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the government is mentioned as funding conservation, not as banning excavation; the choice to leave sites is the archaeologists' own

rewrites.newText: They are afraid that digging further might somehow disturb the volcano and trigger another eruption
rewrites.targetsMisreading: illogical fear about the volcano unsupported by the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage explains sites are left for future technology, not out of any fear of triggering the volcano

rewrites.newText: Amusement at how strange and different the ancient Romans looked
rewrites.targetsMisreading: inappropriate light-hearted reaction to solemn casts
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the casts are described as haunting and emotional evidence of people dying, which invites sadness, not amusement

rewrites.newText: Confusion about what the mysterious plaster casts are actually meant to represent
rewrites.targetsMisreading: assuming the casts are baffling
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage clearly explains the casts show bodies in their final positions, so a visitor would understand, not be confused

rewrites.newText: Boredom because the casts are just dull grey shapes made of plaster
rewrites.targetsMisreading: dismissive reaction contradicting the text's emphasis
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the passage calls the casts the most powerful and emotional evidence of the disaster, so boredom is the opposite of the intended effect

rewrites.newText: The eruption carried on all through the night and into the following early morning
rewrites.targetsMisreading: mistaking a clearly supported statement for the unsupported one
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 4 states the eruption continued throughout the night and into the early hours of 25th August, so this IS supported and cannot be the correct answer

rewrites.newText: The towering column of ash and gas rose more than thirty kilometres up into the sky
rewrites.targetsMisreading: mistaking a clearly supported statement for the unsupported one
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 2 states the column climbed over thirty kilometres into the sky, so this IS supported by the passage

rewrites.newText: To show that daily life in a Roman city like Pompeii was dull and boring
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading of the ordinary activities as evidence life was boring
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the everyday scenes set up a contrast with the coming disaster; they are not there to suggest Roman life was dull

rewrites.newText: To prove that the people of Pompeii had been warned about the volcano but ignored it
rewrites.targetsMisreading: contradiction — inventing a warning that was ignored
rewrites.wrongnessReason: paragraph 1 says most Pompeians did not even realise Vesuvius was a volcano, so they were not warned and could not have ignored it

rewrites.newText: To explain in careful detail what a typical prosperous Roman city like Pompeii looked like
rewrites.targetsMisreading: informational purpose mistaken for the author's real aim
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the opening's function is to contrast normal life with the disaster; describing a typical city is a side effect, not the purpose

rewrites.newText: To demonstrate that ordinary Romans lived their daily lives in much the same way as modern people do
rewrites.targetsMisreading: the graffiti point from paragraph 10 misapplied to the opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the idea that Romans were like modern people comes from the graffiti later on; the opening's job is to set up the contrast with catastrophe

rewrites.newText: Because the sudden eruption was completely unexpected by everyone living in the city
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusing 'paradoxically' with 'unexpectedly'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'paradoxically' signals two opposite outcomes (destruction and preservation), not that the eruption was a surprise

rewrites.newText: Because the author strongly disagrees with what the novelist Robert Harris wrote about the city
rewrites.targetsMisreading: assuming the quoted writer is being challenged
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the author quotes Robert Harris approvingly to support the point; 'paradoxically' marks the destruction-preservation contradiction, not a disagreement

rewrites.newText: Because the many tourists who visit the site both help to fund it and slowly damage it
rewrites.targetsMisreading: a real paradox from paragraph 11 misplaced onto the wrong sentence
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the tourism help-and-harm tension is genuine but appears elsewhere; here 'paradoxically' specifically refers to the disaster both destroying and preserving Pompeii

rewrites.newText: Her mother, who kept insisting the move would be brilliant, had ordered her to sort them
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail misattributed to the wrong character
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage shows Aisha unpacking her own room; her mum gives no such instruction.

rewrites.newText: She wanted to be able to find any particular book quickly once homework started at her new school
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal practical reading true-in-world but unsupported
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The text frames the alphabetising as emotional comfort, not efficiency or homework.

rewrites.newText: Because the word relentless is a gentle, soothing term describing a naturally warm and patient mother
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong definition of the vocabulary word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Relentless means unceasing and hard to stop, not gentle; it signals forced cheer.

rewrites.newText: Because her mother repeated the phrase deliberately to irritate Aisha and wear down her objections to moving
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right topic, wrong motive
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 says her mum was trying to convince herself, not deliberately annoy Aisha.

rewrites.newText: Because her mother had always been a naturally cheerful person, long before the family ever decided to move
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported trait inference
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The cheerfulness is recent and effortful, not a lifelong natural disposition.

rewrites.newText: Because her mother was genuinely delighted about the move to the new town and could not hide her joy
rewrites.targetsMisreading: surface reading that reverses the author's point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Relentless' plus 'trying to convince herself' means the happiness is forced, not genuine.

rewrites.newText: Quiet, reserved, and too shy to approach new people
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite trait
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Meg introduces herself unprompted and chatters constantly — the reverse of quiet and shy.

rewrites.newText: Popular and sporty, endlessly competing to win at everything
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported trait swap
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Meg is artistic, not sporty; paragraph 11 says the group were not the most popular.

rewrites.newText: Nervous and careful, easily upset whenever anything goes slightly wrong
rewrites.targetsMisreading: trait belonging to a different character
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This describes Aisha, not Meg, whose unselfconscious enthusiasm shows confidence.

rewrites.newText: They all share a deep love of art and painting murals together
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong shared interest generalised from one detail
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Only Meg works on the mural; Omar likes chess, Priya poetry, Lucas Rubik's cubes.

rewrites.newText: They are all very similar, sharing the same hobbies and the same personalities
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the correct point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The friends have deliberately varied, distinct interests; the point is individuality.

rewrites.newText: She gradually stops noticing the uniform altogether, which shows that her new friends have distracted her so completely that school itself no longer matters
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible-sounding inference the text never licenses
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The blazer changes from a 'costume' to feeling like it 'belongs to her' — active belonging, not indifference.

rewrites.newText: She begins customising and decorating the blazer to express her individuality, which shows that her growing confidence has made her want to stand out
rewrites.targetsMisreading: fabricated detail
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage never says Aisha alters the blazer; the change is internal.

rewrites.newText: She adores the maroon blazer from the very first morning, which shows that she was genuinely excited about starting at her new school all along
rewrites.targetsMisreading: no-change reading with the wrong starting point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: At the start the uniform felt like a costume for a part she had not auditioned for — dread, not love.

rewrites.newText: She hates the stiff new uniform at the beginning and goes on hating it right to the end, which shows that she never truly settles in
rewrites.targetsMisreading: no-change negative reading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: By paragraph 11 the blazer feels like it belonged to her and she does settle in.

rewrites.newText: The struggles that parents face when they move their children to a completely new town
rewrites.targetsMisreading: minor characters mistaken for the theme
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage centres on Aisha's own experience of finding belonging; the parents are background.

rewrites.newText: How becoming skilled at painting and art can help a nervous child succeed at school
rewrites.targetsMisreading: surface setting mistaken for theme
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Art is merely the setting; Aisha cannot paint and the story is about friendship and belonging.

rewrites.newText: The idea that moving house is always a serious mistake that families should try hard to avoid
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-general negative moral
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage ends positively with Aisha settled and happy, not presenting the move as a mistake.

rewrites.newText: Sam is practical and hard-working, while Jess is lazy and unwilling to make any real effort
rewrites.targetsMisreading: unsupported trait (laziness)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Jess is energetic and drives the adventure — the opposite of lazy.

rewrites.newText: Sam loves being outdoors on adventures, while Jess would always rather stay safely at home indoors
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reversed preference
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Jess is eager for the outdoor adventure; Sam is the reluctant one, so preferences are swapped.

rewrites.newText: He had completely forgotten the cave existed in the many years since discovering it
rewrites.targetsMisreading: forgetting inference
rewrites.wrongnessReason: His journal note 'Must return with proper equipment' shows he clearly remembered.

rewrites.newText: The passage sets out his exact reasons clearly and in careful, complete detail
rewrites.targetsMisreading: meta non-answer that dodges the actual reason
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This gives no reason at all, and the text only implies the cause through a brief note.

rewrites.newText: He was frightened of the dark, unexplored passage and never dared to venture inside
rewrites.targetsMisreading: fear misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He was a professional geologist who mapped caves; the note attributes the delay to lacking equipment.

rewrites.newText: He was secretly hoping to sell the valuable cave to the government for a large sum
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented motive
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage never mentions selling; he intended to return himself with proper equipment.

rewrites.newText: The cave was formed only very recently, in the last few years, by fast-moving water that is still carving the rock
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the timescale stated
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Thousands of years ago' signals great age, contradicting a recent formation.

rewrites.newText: Water still flows regularly through the cave today, which is exactly what made the rock beneath their feet feel so smooth
rewrites.targetsMisreading: present-tense misreading of a past process
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The water had long since found a different route and the passage is cool and dry.

rewrites.newText: The floor is dangerous because the constant water has left it wet and slippery, making it very easy for the children to fall
rewrites.targetsMisreading: hazard detail borrowed from the wrong location
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The slippery surface was the ledge outside; inside, the passage is explicitly cool and dry.

rewrites.newText: She was afraid that someone hiding deeper inside the cave might overhear them and discover them
rewrites.targetsMisreading: fear-of-being-heard misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The whisper expresses awe at the beauty; no one else is present and the passage conveys wonder.

rewrites.newText: Sam had firmly told her to keep her voice down so they would not attract attention
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misattributed instruction
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Sam gives no such instruction; the whisper is Jess's own spontaneous reaction of reverence.

rewrites.newText: By bribing him with sweets and promising to share her pocket money afterwards
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented bribery method
rewrites.wrongnessReason: No bribe is offered; she persuaded him by appealing to what Grandpa Arthur would have wanted.

rewrites.newText: By threatening to go into the dark cave alone if he refused to come with her
rewrites.targetsMisreading: coercion misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Jess uses no threat; her persuasion is emotional, invoking their grandfather's wishes.

rewrites.newText: By promising him that exploring the cave would be completely safe and free of any real danger
rewrites.targetsMisreading: false-reassurance misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: She never claims it is safe; both know it is risky.

rewrites.newText: By telling him that their parents had already given full permission for the two of them to explore
rewrites.targetsMisreading: permission misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 7 says it was exactly the sort of thing their parents would forbid.

rewrites.newText: He has completely overcome every one of his fears and now feels no anxiety whatsoever about any kind of danger
rewrites.targetsMisreading: overstatement of the change
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The 'sensible thing' still pulls at him; he remains cautious, so his fears are moderated, not erased.

rewrites.newText: He feels a heavy sense of duty to finish the exploration that his grandfather began but was never able to complete
rewrites.targetsMisreading: obligation misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He continues because the wonder makes the sensible option unpersuasive — willingness, not felt duty.

rewrites.newText: He has become completely reckless and no longer cares at all about the danger, throwing away the caution that once defined him
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reckless misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He folded the map and tucked it back — a measured act; he stays cautious but becomes braver.

rewrites.newText: It makes the cave sound dangerous and unstable, as though the heavy stone formations might suddenly collapse onto the children
rewrites.targetsMisreading: danger reading of a beauty description
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The vivid detail evokes wonder and beauty, not menace or instability.

rewrites.newText: It proves that the author must have personally visited a great many real caves in order to describe them so accurately
rewrites.targetsMisreading: author-biography misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The question asks about effect on the reader; vivid writing shows craft, not the author's travels.

rewrites.newText: She has never learned Morse code and cannot read it at all
rewrites.targetsMisreading: ignorance misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 5 says she taught herself Morse and knew enough to recognise the pattern was not Morse.

rewrites.newText: Morse code can only be sent using sound, never using flashes of light
rewrites.targetsMisreading: factual misconception about Morse
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Morse can be signalled by light; her reason is the pattern matches no Morse character.

rewrites.newText: She thinks Morse code is far too old-fashioned for anyone to be using now
rewrites.targetsMisreading: irrelevant reasoning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Her stated reason is the pattern does not correspond to a standard Morse character, not the code's age.

rewrites.newText: The flashing signals appeared far too slowly to possibly be genuine Morse code
rewrites.targetsMisreading: speed misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Speed is never the issue; the mismatch is the pattern maps onto no Morse character.

rewrites.newText: She does not care in the slightest where she lives, feeling equally at home anywhere
rewrites.targetsMisreading: indifference misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: She plainly cares — she had not entirely settled and leans on her hobbies to cope.

rewrites.newText: She absolutely loves the village and has already made a large circle of close, loyal friends there
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite social situation
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The other children treat her with polite wariness and she has not settled.

rewrites.newText: She hates living in Pencarrow so much that she is desperate to move straight back to her old home
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-negative extreme
rewrites.wrongnessReason: She copes through her passions and never expresses hatred or a wish to move back.

rewrites.newText: She finally manages to decipher a hidden Morse code message that spells out the name of the mysterious signaller
rewrites.targetsMisreading: Morse misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage ruled out Morse; the breakthrough is realising the pattern is music.

rewrites.newText: She works out exactly who has been climbing the lighthouse each night to send the mysterious flashing signals across the bay
rewrites.targetsMisreading: premature resolution
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage ends before she learns who; decoding the melody is the breakthrough, not identifying the sender.

rewrites.newText: To make the passage much longer so that it fills more space on the page
rewrites.targetsMisreading: length/padding misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The gradual reveal is a deliberate suspense device, not filler.

rewrites.newText: Because the author simply forgot to include all of the details together earlier in the story
rewrites.targetsMisreading: authorial carelessness misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The staggered pacing is intentional craft to build suspense, not an oversight.

rewrites.newText: To test whether the reader is paying close enough attention to remember all the earlier clues
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong authorial purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The purpose is to build tension and mirror the step-by-step investigation, not test memory.

rewrites.newText: Because Noor herself was confused by the pattern and needed a long time to think it all through
rewrites.targetsMisreading: character state confused with authorial choice
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The question asks about the author's structural decision; Noor's confusion is a plot detail.

rewrites.newText: To deliberately frustrate and annoy the reader by refusing to give them any answer
rewrites.targetsMisreading: negative-intent misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A cliffhanger creates intriguing anticipation, the opposite of deliberate frustration.

rewrites.newText: Because the author has not yet decided who is sending the signals from the lighthouse
rewrites.targetsMisreading: author-ignorance misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The withholding is a deliberate cliffhanger technique, not a sign the author is unsure.

rewrites.newText: Because the identity of the person sending the signals does not really matter to the story
rewrites.targetsMisreading: significance misreading
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The sender's identity is the central mystery driving Noor's quest, so it clearly matters.

rewrites.newText: In Trafalgar Square, close to Nelson's Column
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right topic (a famous central-London landmark) but wrong place
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 12 states the statue stands on the Victoria Embankment beside Westminster Bridge.

rewrites.newText: In the gardens outside Buckingham Palace, the royal residence
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible royal London location the text never gives
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 12 locates the statue on the Victoria Embankment; Buckingham Palace is never mentioned.

rewrites.newText: Because Roman law compelled every conquered king to leave part of his kingdom to the emperor
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible inference the passage never licenses (legal compulsion)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 calls the bequest a voluntary gesture of loyalty; no legal requirement is stated.

rewrites.newText: It sat close to the supply base where his legions were provisioned
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented military convenience
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 9 says he chose the ground for its confined space and forest cover; no supply base is mentioned.

rewrites.newText: It lay near London, making it quick for his forces to reach
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from the wrong paragraph (London, paragraph 7)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The final battle was in the Midlands along Watling Street, not near London.

rewrites.newText: The valley contained fresh streams that would keep his ten thousand soldiers supplied
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible but unsupported practical detail (water)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 9 attributes his choice to the confining terrain, not water supplies.

rewrites.newText: He intended to surround Boudicca's warriors from behind and cut off their retreat
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misreading of paragraph 10 (the escape was blocked by the Britons' OWN wagons)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He chose the valley to limit how many warriors could engage; the retreat was blocked by Boudicca's own wagons.

rewrites.newText: That the archaeological evidence from Colchester is confused and cannot be trusted
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of what the physical evidence shows
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The burnt destruction layer is reliable proof of a great fire, not that the archaeology is untrustworthy.

rewrites.newText: That Boudicca's warriors must have been armed with unusually advanced weapons for the period
rewrites.targetsMisreading: inference the passage never licenses from burnt debris
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A layer of burnt debris proves fire, not weapon technology.

rewrites.newText: That Colchester was originally built and settled by the Romans as a colony for soldiers
rewrites.targetsMisreading: true-in-the-text but not what the destruction layer proves
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The Romans did found Colchester, but the destruction layer proves a fire, not who built the town.

rewrites.newText: He had been outwitted and deceived by one of Boudicca's clever schemes into abandoning the city
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented trickery not present in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 7 calls the retreat a cold but calculated decision he made himself; Boudicca did not trick him.

rewrites.newText: He was a frightened coward who fled from the approaching danger to save only his own life
rewrites.targetsMisreading: emotional negative reading the neutral facts do not support
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He ordered an organised retreat and advised civilians to leave, acting strategically rather than cowardly.

rewrites.newText: A determination to defend the ancient Celtic religion and its priests against the new beliefs the Romans were imposing
rewrites.targetsMisreading: true-of-the-era motive but unsupported by THIS text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 3 gives her motive as fury at being flogged, her daughters assaulted, and the kingdom seized; religion is never named.

rewrites.newText: Because the enormous bronze statue is the largest and most impressive monument anywhere in the whole of London
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal focus on size instead of symbolic meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 12 makes the placement symbolic because of irony, not size.

rewrites.newText: Because the Houses of Parliament are the most visited and most photographed tourist building in the entire city of London
rewrites.targetsMisreading: irrelevant tourism fact substituted for the symbolic point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The symbolism lies in the irony of Boudicca watching over the seat of government, not visitor numbers.

rewrites.newText: Because Boudicca is remembered and honoured there as the very first woman ever to take her seat inside the English Parliament
rewrites.targetsMisreading: factually impossible claim (she lived c. 60 AD)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Boudicca lived around 60 AD, centuries before Parliament existed; the placement is symbolic.

rewrites.newText: Because the same craftsmen and architects who constructed the Houses of Parliament were also responsible for designing and casting the statue
rewrites.targetsMisreading: irrelevant construction connection not in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The symbolism is the irony of her placement; no link between the makers is stated.

rewrites.newText: To explain in detail the archaeological methods used to excavate the destruction layer at Colchester
rewrites.targetsMisreading: one small detail mistaken for the whole purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The destruction layer is a single supporting detail; the purpose is a balanced account of the rebellion.

rewrites.newText: To argue forcefully that the Roman invasion of Britain was wrong and should never have happened
rewrites.targetsMisreading: one-sided argument mistaken for a balanced account
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The author is balanced, even flagging Roman propaganda; the passage does not argue the invasion was wrong.

rewrites.newText: To explain why Roman civilisation was far more advanced and superior to the culture of the Celtic tribes
rewrites.targetsMisreading: cultural-superiority claim the passage never makes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage is sympathetic to Boudicca and never argues Roman culture was superior.

rewrites.newText: It creates a peaceful and calm atmosphere that gently settles the reader before the story begins
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite tone to the dramatic opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The opening describes the empire shaken to its foundations, creating drama, not calm.

rewrites.newText: It bores the reader by opening with a dull historical date instead of any real excitement
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal reaction to the opening year (60 AD)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The date sets up a dramatic contrast between a mighty empire and one woman, designed to grip the reader.

rewrites.newText: Beside the River Thames in the south of England
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong famous English river
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 places the estate near the River Wear in County Durham, not on the Thames.

rewrites.newText: Taking his father's greyhound for a walk
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from the wrong part of the story (the greyhound, paragraphs 8-10)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 says he was fishing; the greyhound belongs to the later battle plan.

rewrites.newText: Exploring a dark cave in the nearby hills
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented setting
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He caught the creature while fishing at the river; no cave is involved.

rewrites.newText: Hunting wild animals in the forest near the estate
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible noble pastime but wrong activity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 shows him fishing, not hunting.

rewrites.newText: Swimming in the cold waters of the River Wear one morning
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right location (the river) but wrong activity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He was fishing, not swimming, when the creature took his line.

rewrites.newText: The greyhound broke free and escaped from the castle before the horn signal was given
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented escape not in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 10 says the plan failed because the father ran out before releasing the dog.

rewrites.newText: The greyhound ran faster than John's father and reached the rock first after the horn
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reversal of the actual order of arrival
rewrites.wrongnessReason: John saw his father first; the greyhound arrived only moments later.

rewrites.newText: John was unable to find his sword after the exhausting battle and could not kill the animal
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented weapon problem
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The plan failed because the father appeared before the dog was released; John did kill the greyhound.

rewrites.newText: He had completely forgotten about the creature he once threw into the well as a boy
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the truth (he confronted it)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 6 says he discovered the devastation and resolved to put it right.

rewrites.newText: He had grown even more reckless and irresponsible than he had been in his wild youth
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the maturation the text describes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 6 describes him as older, wiser and remorseful.

rewrites.newText: His father was protected by thick armour that no ordinary weapon could pierce
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented physical obstacle, misattributing the armour to the father
rewrites.wrongnessReason: It was John who wore the spearhead armour; he spared his father out of love.

rewrites.newText: Because the Worm returned from the river after many years and finally killed the ninth lord
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented survival of the Worm
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The Worm was destroyed in the river; the curse ended by reaching nine generations.

rewrites.newText: Because after the ninth lord died there were no heirs left to continue the Lambton family line
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented extinction of the family line
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The curse was fulfilled because this was the ninth generation, not because the family ran out of heirs.

rewrites.newText: Because the ninth lord was the very first Lambton to die peacefully and quietly in his own bed
rewrites.targetsMisreading: subtle inversion of the curse's terms
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The ninth lord died in his carriage; the curse ended by completing nine generations.

rewrites.newText: Because the ninth lord discovered a powerful magic spell that finally broke the ancient family curse forever
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented magical resolution
rewrites.wrongnessReason: No spell is mentioned; the curse simply expired after nine generations.

rewrites.newText: They are genuinely frightened by the gruesome story and warn readers to take it seriously
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong emotion drawn from the word 'gruesome'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 1 is warm and amused, calling the tale delightfully gruesome; the tone is playful.

rewrites.newText: They believe the old legend is embarrassing and should quietly be forgotten by the local people
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the author's evident enjoyment
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The author celebrates the legend's endurance and relishes it.

rewrites.newText: They firmly believe that every single detail of the legend is completely true and historically accurate
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the author's stated scepticism
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 1 says whether any of it is true is impossible to say.

rewrites.newText: It shows that the father never truly loved John and cared nothing for whether he lived or died
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of what the tears of relief convey
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The father's tears and outstretched arms show overwhelming love and relief.

rewrites.newText: It creates humour because the father's clumsy and comical behaviour makes the reader laugh out loud at the whole scene
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong tone (comic instead of poignant)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The moment is tender and tragic, since the father's rush triggers the curse.

rewrites.newText: Because they had already become extremely rare and needed protection from hunters
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from the wrong period (rarity came centuries later)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: In medieval London kites were among the most common birds; rarity came only with the later decline.

rewrites.newText: A technical scientific paper focused entirely on red kite genetics
rewrites.targetsMisreading: one detail (genetics) mistaken for the text type
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Genetics is a single supporting point; the passage is a general informative account.

rewrites.newText: A fictional adventure story about the rescue of a single bird
rewrites.targetsMisreading: factual account mistaken for fiction
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage reports a real, dated conservation programme with statistics.

rewrites.newText: A persuasive letter written to campaign against the practice of game shooting
rewrites.targetsMisreading: a mentioned topic mistaken for the whole form
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Game shooting is one cause of harm; the passage is a balanced informative account.

rewrites.newText: A travel guide describing places to visit across the beautiful Chiltern Hills
rewrites.targetsMisreading: the setting mistaken for the subject
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The Chilterns are the setting; the passage is about the kite's recovery.

rewrites.newText: Welsh farmers refused to protect the birds and instead drove them away from all their land
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the text (farmers were paid to tolerate them)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 4 says farmers were paid to tolerate the kites; the slow recovery was caused by inbreeding.

rewrites.newText: There were far too many other predators in the remote Welsh valleys that hunted and competed with them
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented predator pressure
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The slow recovery was due to inbreeding and low fertility, not other predators.

rewrites.newText: There was simply not enough food in the remote Welsh oak valleys to support a steadily growing population
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented food shortage
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The cause given is inbreeding and eggs failing to hatch, not a shortage of food.

rewrites.newText: The Welsh kites belonged to a completely different species that could not breed with the others
rewrites.targetsMisreading: false species distinction
rewrites.wrongnessReason: They were the same species; the issue was the small, inbred Welsh population.

rewrites.newText: Importing young kites from Sweden and Spain turned out to be far cheaper than capturing the protected Welsh birds
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented cost motive
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The reason was the genetic health of imported birds, not cost.

rewrites.newText: That kites completely avoid all urban areas and never fly near towns or busy roads
rewrites.targetsMisreading: claim contradicted by the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 10 shows kites circling over motorways; the tracking revealed long-distance travel, not avoidance of towns.

rewrites.newText: The maximum distance a kite is able to carry food back to its nest
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal reading of the word 'carrying'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Carrying capacity is an ecological term for sustainable population size, not distance.

rewrites.newText: The maximum total weight of birds and animals that the land is able to support
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading of 'capacity' as physical weight
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The phrase refers to the maximum number of kites the habitat can feed, not any weight.

rewrites.newText: The amount of pollution and waste that the surrounding habitat is able to safely absorb
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented environmental meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Nothing about pollution is involved; it means the sustainable population of kites.

rewrites.newText: The total number of different bird and animal species that can live together in one shared habitat
rewrites.targetsMisreading: swaps population size of one species for species diversity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: It refers to the maximum number of kites (a single species), not how many species can coexist.

rewrites.newText: Illegal poisoning by gamekeepers on shooting estates
rewrites.targetsMisreading: correct answer here is the one NOT mentioned; this stays a genuinely mentioned threat
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Illegal poisoning IS named as a threat in paragraph 11, so it is not the 'NOT mentioned' answer.

rewrites.newText: Fatal collisions with vehicles on busy roads and motorways
rewrites.targetsMisreading: expanded but still a real, mentioned threat
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 11 names vehicle collisions as a cause of death, so this is genuinely mentioned.

rewrites.newText: Growing competition for food with the expanding buzzard population
rewrites.targetsMisreading: expanded but still a real, mentioned threat
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 11 states kites compete for food with buzzards, so this is mentioned.
